county_metadata.research_tips: County Recorder in Connersville. Created 1818 from Wayne and Franklin counties. Connersville was an early auto manufacturing center — Lexington, McFarlan, and Cord automobile company records. Fayette County Historical Society. Whitewater River valley settlements. Strong Quaker presence — Connersville Monthly Meeting records. BLM-GLO federal land patents. Whitewater Canal passed through county — canal records at Indiana State Archives. Check Wayne and Franklin counties for pre-1818 records. Nulltown and Everton area records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1818. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Indiana deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — earliest deeds may reference BLM-GLO original patents.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival — continuous records from county formation

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage applications, licenses, and returns from 1818. Indiana County Clerk issues marriage licenses. Applications include ages, birthplaces, and parents' names (improving detail over time).
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well-preserved at County Clerk office

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, guardianships, and estate inventories from 1818. Indiana probate is handled by the Circuit Court (County Clerk). Inventories often list personal property in detail and name heirs.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Probate records generally well-preserved at county level

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil and criminal case files, order books, and judgments from 1818. Indiana Circuit Court is the general trial court. Early records include debt cases, property disputes, bastardy cases, and naturalization petitions (pre-1906) that identify family relationships. Order books often record road petitions naming landowners.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Court records generally well-preserved at county level

source_profiles.repository: Fayette County Auditor, Connersville / Indiana State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Tax duplicates list property owners, land descriptions, and property values from 1818. Indiana county auditor records. Early tax lists serve as census substitutes. Indiana State Archives holds some microfilmed county tax records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Some tax duplicates at Indiana State Archives; others remain at county level

source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Original federal land patents for Fayette County. Indiana is a federal land state — most land was sold through federal land offices. Patents record first purchaser from the government. Searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov. Cross-reference with county deed records for subsequent transfers.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete federal records — well-preserved and digitized at BLM-GLO

source_profiles.source_name: Society of Friends (Quaker) Meeting Records — Fayette County
source_profiles.repository: Earlham College Friends Collection, Richmond / Indiana Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Monthly meeting records include births, marriages, deaths, removals (transfers), and disownments. Indiana Yearly Meeting and Western Yearly Meeting records. Quakers kept meticulous records. Indiana was a major destination for Quaker migration from the Carolinas and Virginia — check origin meeting records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Exceptionally well-preserved; many microfilmed by FamilySearch and held at Earlham College

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wabash and Erie Canal (longest canal ever built in North America, 468 miles) and Whitewater Canal construction records, land purchases, worker rolls, and canal-related property records. Canal workers included many Irish immigrants. Canal land grants at Indiana State Archives.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Canal Commission records at Indiana State Archives; some published by Indiana Historical Bureau

county_metadata.research_tips: County Recorder in New Albany. Created 1819 from Clark and Harrison counties. New Albany was one of Indiana's largest cities in the mid-1800s — major steamboat building center on the Ohio River. Floyd County Historical Society. Steamboat construction records and river commerce records document worker families. New Albany was briefly the state's largest city. Clark's Grant territory. Strong German, Irish, and African American communities. Cross-reference with Louisville/Jefferson County, KY. Scribner House Museum. Underground Railroad activity documented along the river.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1819. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Indiana deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — earliest deeds may reference BLM-GLO original patents.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival — continuous records from county formation

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage applications, licenses, and returns from 1819. Indiana County Clerk issues marriage licenses. Applications include ages, birthplaces, and parents' names (improving detail over time).
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well-preserved at County Clerk office

source_profiles.repository: Floyd County Clerk (Circuit/Superior Court), New Albany
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, guardianships, and estate inventories from 1819. Indiana probate is handled by the Circuit Court (County Clerk). Inventories often list personal property in detail and name heirs.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Probate records generally well-preserved at county level

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil and criminal case files, order books, and judgments from 1819. Indiana Circuit Court is the general trial court. Early records include debt cases, property disputes, bastardy cases, and naturalization petitions (pre-1906) that identify family relationships. Order books often record road petitions naming landowners.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Court records generally well-preserved at county level

source_profiles.repository: Floyd County Auditor, New Albany / Indiana State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Tax duplicates list property owners, land descriptions, and property values from 1819. Indiana county auditor records. Early tax lists serve as census substitutes. Indiana State Archives holds some microfilmed county tax records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Some tax duplicates at Indiana State Archives; others remain at county level

source_profiles.source_name: Clark's Grant Land Records — Floyd County
source_profiles.repository: Indiana State Archives, Indianapolis / Indiana State Library
source_profiles.coverage_notes: George Rogers Clark's Illinois Regiment land grant (150,000 acres) in southeastern Indiana. Soldiers who served under Clark in the Revolutionary War received land warrants. This area was NOT part of the federal land system — use Virginia/Illinois records, not BLM-GLO. Early Clarksville (1784) was Indiana's first American settlement.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Records at Indiana State Archives and Indiana State Library; some published in Indiana land record compilations

source_profiles.source_name: Underground Railroad and African American Records — Floyd County
source_profiles.repository: Indiana State Archives / Indiana Historical Society / Levi Coffin House
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Indiana was a free state but had Black Codes restricting African American settlement. Court records include freedom suits, fugitive slave cases, and Black Code enforcement. Levi Coffin of Fountain City (Wayne County) was the 'President of the Underground Railroad.' Quaker meeting records document abolitionist activity.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Scattered across local historical societies and Indiana State Archives; some published accounts

source_profiles.source_name: Ohio River Commerce Records — Floyd County
source_profiles.repository: Indiana State Archives / Local historical societies / Kentucky river communities
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Steamboat records, ferry licenses, river pilot records, and flatboat commerce. Ohio River counties had close economic ties to Kentucky — cross-reference Kentucky county records across the river. Early Indiana settlement came primarily via the Ohio River.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Ferry licenses at county level; steamboat records at NARA and historical societies

source_profiles.repository: Floyd County Clerk, New Albany / NARA Chicago
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Declarations of intention, petitions for naturalization, and certificates. Pre-1906 records at county Circuit Court (County Clerk). Post-1906 copies at NARA Chicago. Indiana had significant German, Irish, Swiss, and later Eastern European and Mexican immigration.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Many Indiana naturalization records indexed on FamilySearch and Ancestry; some at Indiana State Archives

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Indiana provided approximately 197,000 soldiers to the Union Army. Adjutant General records, enlistment papers, and discharge records at Indiana State Archives. Indiana Soldiers and Sailors Monument (Indianapolis). W.H.H. Terrell's 'Report of the Adjutant General of the State of Indiana' (8 vols.) published rosters.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Indiana Adjutant General records well-preserved at Indiana State Archives; indexed online

county_metadata.research_tips: County Recorder in Covington. Created 1825 from Montgomery County. Wabash River and Coal Creek valley settlements. Fountain County Historical Society. Portland Arch Nature Preserve area. Attica was an important Wabash River crossing and rail junction — railroad employment records. BLM-GLO federal land patents. Strong Methodist and Presbyterian church records. Early settlers from Kentucky and Virginia. Check Montgomery County for pre-1825 records. Veedersburg and Mellott area records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1825. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Indiana deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — earliest deeds may reference BLM-GLO original patents.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival — continuous records from county formation

source_profiles.survival_notes: Well-preserved at County Clerk office

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, guardianships, and estate inventories from 1825. Indiana probate is handled by the Circuit Court (County Clerk). Inventories often list personal property in detail and name heirs.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Probate records generally well-preserved at county level

Order books often record road petitions naming landowners.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Original federal land patents for Fountain County. Indiana is a federal land state — most land was sold through federal land offices. source_profiles.repository: Indiana State Archives / Indiana Historical Society / Levi Coffin House
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Indiana was a free state but had Black Codes restricting African American settlement. Court records include freedom suits, fugitive slave cases, and Black Code enforcement. Levi Coffin of Fountain City (Wayne County) was the 'President of the Underground Railroad.' Quaker meeting records document abolitionist activity.

county_metadata.research_tips: County Recorder in Brookville. Created 1810 from Clark, Dearborn, and Jefferson counties. Brookville was Indiana's territorial and early state capital candidate; the Whitewater Valley was one of the first settled areas. Franklin County Historical Society. Strong German Catholic heritage — Oldenburg (Village of Spires) has exceptional parish records. Immaculate Conception Convent (Sisters of St. Francis, 1851) archives. Whitewater Canal records. BLM-GLO federal land patents. Brookville Reservoir area. Batesville and Cedar Grove records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1810. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Indiana deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — earliest deeds may reference BLM-GLO original patents.

source_profiles.source_name: German Catholic Parish Records — Franklin County
source_profiles.repository: Archdiocese of Indianapolis / Diocese of Evansville / Parish offices
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Baptism, marriage, and burial records from German Catholic parishes. Many parishes kept records in Latin or German. Parish registers often include parents' birthplaces in Germany. St. Meinrad Archabbey (Dubois/Spencer) holds significant church records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Parish records generally well-preserved; some microfilmed by FamilySearch

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wabash and Erie Canal (longest canal ever built in North America, 468 miles) and Whitewater Canal construction records, land purchases, worker rolls, and canal-related property records. Canal workers included many Irish immigrants. Canal land grants at Indiana State Archives.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Canal Commission records at Indiana State Archives; some published by Indiana Historical Bureau

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Declarations of intention, petitions for naturalization, and certificates. Pre-1906 records at county Circuit Court (County Clerk). Post-1906 copies at NARA Chicago. Indiana had significant German, Irish, Swiss, and later Eastern European and Mexican immigration.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1835 from Allen, Cass, St. Joseph.
county_metadata.research_tips: County Recorder in Rochester. Created 1835 from Allen, Cass, and St. Joseph counties. Fulton County Historical Society in Rochester has extensive collections including the Round Barn Museum. Tippecanoe River and Lake Manitou area settlements. BLM-GLO federal land patents. Strong Potawatomi presence before removal — Potawatomi Trail of Death (1838) records at NARA and Indiana State Archives. Akron and Kewanna area records. Early settlers from Ohio and Pennsylvania. Check parent counties for pre-1835 records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1835. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Indiana deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — earliest deeds may reference BLM-GLO original patents.

source_profiles.repository: NARA, Washington DC / Indiana State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Potawatomi Nation records including treaties, Trail of Death removal records (1838), land allotments, and agent correspondence. 859 Potawatomi were forcibly removed from Indiana to Kansas in September 1838. Chief Menominee's band. Pokagon Band of Potawatomi retained some lands.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Federal records at NARA Record Group 75; Trail of Death records well-documented

county_metadata.research_tips: County Recorder in Princeton. Created 1813 from Knox County. Gibson County is in the southwestern Wabash Lowlands — early settlement area tied to Vincennes. Gibson County Historical Society. Princeton and Fort Branch area records. Oil production in the 1880s brought workers. Patoka River valley. BLM-GLO federal land patents. Strong Southern-origin settler population — many from Kentucky, Virginia, and North Carolina. Check Knox County for pre-1813 records. Methodist and Baptist church records from early settlement period.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1813. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Indiana deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — earliest deeds may reference BLM-GLO original patents.

Order books often record road petitions naming landowners.

county_metadata.research_tips: County Recorder in Marion, IN (not to be confused with Indianapolis/Marion County). Created 1831 from Delaware and Madison counties. Gas City and Marion boomed during the Indiana natural gas boom (1880s-1890s) — factory and industrial records. Grant County Historical Society. Mississinewa River valley — Battle of the Mississinewa (1812) records at NARA. Miami Indian presence — check Miami removal records. Marion is the county seat. BLM-GLO federal land patents. James Dean birthplace (Marion). Easter Pageant tradition.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1831. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Indiana deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — earliest deeds may reference BLM-GLO original patents.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival — continuous records from county formation

source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Original federal land patents for Grant County. Indiana is a federal land state — most land was sold through federal land offices. Patents record first purchaser from the government. Searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov. Cross-reference with county deed records for subsequent transfers.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete federal records — well-preserved and digitized at BLM-GLO

source_profiles.repository: NARA, Washington DC / Indiana State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Miami Nation records including treaties, removal records (1846), land allotments, annuity rolls, and agent correspondence. Some Miami families received individual land allotments and remained in Indiana. Chief Richardville (Peshewah) and Meshingomesia's band records. Treaty of St. Mary's (1818), Treaty of Paradise Spring (1826).
source_profiles.survival_notes: Federal records at NARA Record Group 75; some at Indiana State Archives

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Indiana provided approximately 197,000 soldiers to the Union Army. Adjutant General records, enlistment papers, and discharge records at Indiana State Archives. Indiana Soldiers and Sailors Monument (Indianapolis). W.H.H. Terrell's 'Report of the Adjutant General of the State of Indiana' (8 vols.) published rosters.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Indiana Adjutant General records well-preserved at Indiana State Archives; indexed online

county_metadata.research_tips: County Recorder in Bloomfield. Created 1821 from Daviess and Sullivan counties. Major coal mining county — coal mine employment records document immigrant workers. Greene County Historical Society in Bloomfield. Linton was a significant mining town — Italian, Eastern European, and African American miners. White River and Eel River valleys. BLM-GLO federal land patents. Worthington and Jasonville area records. Strong labor movement records — United Mine Workers activity. Check Daviess and Sullivan for pre-1821 records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1821. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Indiana deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — earliest deeds may reference BLM-GLO original patents.

source_profiles.source_name: Coal Mining Employment Records — Greene County
source_profiles.repository: Indiana State Archives / Indiana Coal Council / Local historical societies
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Indiana coal mine employment records, company town records, and United Mine Workers (UMW) union records. Coal mining attracted Italian, Eastern European, and African American workers. Mine inspection reports at Indiana State Archives list workers. Company store ledgers document families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Mine records scattered; Indiana Bureau of Mines reports at Indiana State Archives

county_metadata.research_tips: County Recorder in Noblesville. Created 1823 from unorganized territory (Delaware). One of Indiana's fastest-growing counties (Indianapolis suburbs). Hamilton County Historical Society in Noblesville. Conner Prairie Interactive History Park documents early settlement. White River valley. BLM-GLO federal land patents. Early Quaker settlement in Westfield — Friends meeting records. Noblesville, Carmel, Fishers, and Westfield area records. Well-preserved county records from 1823. County Clerk holds marriage records from 1833.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1823. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Indiana deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — earliest deeds may reference BLM-GLO original patents.

county_metadata.research_tips: County Recorder in Greenfield. Created 1827 from Madison County. Birthplace of poet James Whitcomb Riley (Greenfield). Hancock County Historical Society. Brandywine Creek and Sugar Creek valley settlements. BLM-GLO federal land patents. National Road (US 40) passed through the county — early tavern and road records. Strong Methodist and Baptist church records. Greenfield and New Palestine area records. Check Madison County for pre-1827 records. Well-preserved county records from 1828.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1827. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Indiana deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — earliest deeds may reference BLM-GLO original patents.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival — continuous records from county formation

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage applications, licenses, and returns from 1827. Indiana County Clerk issues marriage licenses. Applications include ages, birthplaces, and parents' names (improving detail over time).
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well-preserved at County Clerk office

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, guardianships, and estate inventories from 1827. Indiana probate is handled by the Circuit Court (County Clerk). Inventories often list personal property in detail and name heirs.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Probate records generally well-preserved at county level

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil and criminal case files, order books, and judgments from 1827. Indiana Circuit Court is the general trial court. Early records include debt cases, property disputes, bastardy cases, and naturalization petitions (pre-1906) that identify family relationships. Order books often record road petitions naming landowners.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Court records generally well-preserved at county level

source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Original federal land patents for Hancock County. Indiana is a federal land state — most land was sold through federal land offices. Patents record first purchaser from the government. Searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov. Cross-reference with county deed records for subsequent transfers.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete federal records — well-preserved and digitized at BLM-GLO
